lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Good dawning to thee, friend: art of this house?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Ay.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Where may we set our horses?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I’ the mire.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Prithee, if thou lovest me, tell me.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I love thee not.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Why, then, I care not for thee.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: If I had thee in Lipsbury pinfold, I would make thee care for me.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Why dost thou use me thus? I know thee not.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Fellow, I know thee.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: What dost thou know me for?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: A knave; a rascal; an eater of broken meats; a base, proud,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: shallow, beggarly, three-suited, hundred-pound, filthy, worsted-stocking knave; a lily-livered, action-taking knave, a

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: whoreson, glass-gazing, super-serviceable finical rogue; one-trunk-inheriting slave; one that wouldst be a

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: bawd, in way of good service, and art nothing but the composition

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: of a knave, beggar, coward, pandar, and the son and heir of

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: a mongrel bitch: one whom I will beat into clamorous whining, if

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: thou deniest the least syllable of thy addition.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Why, what a monstrous fellow art thou, thus to rail on one

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: that is neither known of thee nor knows thee!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: What a brazen-faced varlet art thou, to deny thou knowest me! Is

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: it two days ago since I tripped up thy heels, and beat

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: thee before the king? Draw, you rogue: for, though it be night,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: yet the moon shines; I’ll make a sop o’ the moonshine of

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: you: draw, you whoreson cullionly barber-monger, draw.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Away! I have nothing to do with thee.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Draw, you rascal: you come with letters against the king; and take

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: vanity the puppet’s part against the royalty of her father: draw, you

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: rogue, or I’ll so carbonado your shanks: draw, you rascal; come your

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: ways.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Help, ho! murder! help!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Strike, you slave; stand, rogue, stand; you neat slave, strike. Beating him.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Help, ho! murder! murder!

lines.speaker: EDMUND
lines.text: How now! What’s the matter?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: With you, goodman boy, an you please: come, I’ll flesh ye; come on, young master.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Weapons! arms! What’s the matter here?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Keep peace, upon your lives: He dies that strikes again. What is the matter?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: The messengers from our sister and the king.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: What is your difference? speak.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: I am scarce in breath, my lord.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: No marvel, you have so bestirred your valour. You cowardly rascal, nature

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: disclaims in thee: a tailor made thee.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Thou art a strange fellow: a tailor make a man?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Ay, a tailor, sir: a stone-cutter or painter could not have made

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: him so ill, though he had been but two hours at the

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: trade.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Speak yet, how grew your quarrel?

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: This ancient ruffian, sir, whose life I have spared at suit of his gray beard—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thou whoreson zed! thou unnecessary letter! My lord, if you will give

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: me leave, I will tread this unbolted villain into mortar, and daub

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: the wall of a jakes with him. Spare my gray beard, you

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: wagtail?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Peace, sirrah!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: You beastly knave, know you no reverence?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Yes, sir; but anger hath a privilege.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Why art thou angry?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That such a slave as this should wear a sword,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who wears no honesty. Such smiling rogues as these,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Like rats, oft bite the holy cords a-twain

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Which are too intrinse t’ unloose; smooth every passion

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That in the natures of their lords rebel;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Bring oil to fire, snow to their colder moods;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Renege, affirm, and turn their halcyon beaks

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: With every gale and vary of their masters,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Knowing nought, like dogs, but following.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: A plague upon your epileptic visage!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Smile you my speeches, as I were a fool?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Goose, if I had you upon Sarum plain,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I’ld drive ye cackling home to Camelot.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Why, art thou mad, old fellow?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: How fell you out? say that.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: No contraries hold more antipathy

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Than I and such a knave.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Why dost thou call him a knave? What’s his offence?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: His countenance likes me not.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: No more, perchance, does mine, nor his, nor hers.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Sir, ’tis my occupation to be plain:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I have seen better faces in my time

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Than stands on any shoulder that I see

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Before me at this instant.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: This is some fellow,

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Who, having been praised for bluntness, doth affect

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: A saucy roughness, and constrains the garb

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Quite from his nature: he cannot flatter, he,

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: An honest mind and plain, he must speak truth!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: An they will take it, so; if not, he’s plain.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: These kind of knaves I know, which in this plainness

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Harbour more craft and more corrupter ends

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Than twenty silly ducking observants

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: That stretch their duties nicely.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Sir, in good sooth, in sincere verity,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Under the allowance of your great aspect,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Whose influence, like the wreath of radiant fire

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: On flickering Phoebus’ front—

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: What mean’st by this?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To go out of my dialect, which you discommend so much. I

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: know, sir, I am no flatterer: he that beguiled you in a

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: plain accent was a plain knave; which for my part I will

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: not be, though I should win your displeasure to entreat me to

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: What was the offence you gave him?

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: I never gave him any:

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: It pleased the king his master very late

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: To strike at me, upon his misconstruction;

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: When he, conjunct and flattering his displeasure,

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Tripp’d me behind; being down, insulted, rail’d,

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: And put upon him such a deal of man,

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: That worthied him, got praises of the king

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: For him attempting who was self-subdued;

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: And, in the fleshment of this dread exploit,

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Drew on me here again.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: None of these rogues and cowards

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: But Ajax is their fool.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Fetch forth the stocks!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: You stubborn ancient knave, you reverend braggart,

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: We’ll teach you—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Sir, I am too old to learn:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Call not your stocks for me: I serve the king;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: On whose employment I was sent to you:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: You shall do small respect, show too bold malice

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Against the grace and person of my master,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Stocking his messenger.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Fetch forth the stocks! As I have life and honour,

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: There shall he sit till noon.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Till noon! till night, my lord; and all night too.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Why, madam, if I were your father’s dog,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: You should not use me so.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Sir, being his knave, I will.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: This is a fellow of the self-same colour

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Our sister speaks of. Come, bring away the stocks! Stocks brought out.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Let me beseech your grace not to do so:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: His fault is much, and the good king his master

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Will cheque him for ’t: your purposed low correction

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is such as basest and contemned’st wretches

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For pilferings and most common trespasses

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Are punish’d with: the king must take it ill,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That he’s so slightly valued in his messenger,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Should have him thus restrain’d.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: I’ll answer that.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: My sister may receive it much more worse,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: To have her gentleman abused, assaulted,

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: For following her affairs. Put in his legs. Kent is put in the stocks.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Come, my good lord, away.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I am sorry for thee, friend; ’tis the duke’s pleasure,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Whose disposition, all the world well knows,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Will not be rubb’d nor stopp’d: I’ll entreat for thee.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Pray, do not, sir: I have watched and travell’d hard;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Some time I shall sleep out, the rest I’ll whistle.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: A good man’s fortune may grow out at heels:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Give you good morrow!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The duke’s to blame in this; ’twill be ill taken.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Good king, that must approve the common saw,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Thou out of heaven’s benediction comest

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To the warm sun!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Approach, thou beacon to this under globe,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That by thy comfortable beams I may

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Peruse this letter! Nothing almost sees miracles

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: But misery: I know ’tis from Cordelia,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who hath most fortunately been inform’d

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Of my obscured course; and shall find time

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: From this enormous state, seeking to give

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Losses their remedies. All weary and o’erwatch’d,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Take vantage, heavy eyes, not to behold

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This shameful lodging.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Fortune, good night: smile once more: turn thy wheel! Sleeps.

stage_directions.text: Enter Edmund, with his rapier drawn, Cornwall, Regan, Gloucester, and Servants.
Exeunt all but Gloucester and Kent.

glosses.definition: early morning
rascal — Kent's volley of insults to Oswald

glosses.phrase: knave; a rascal; an eater of broken meats
glosses.definition: Kent's Homeric string of insults for Oswald — 'broken meats' = leftover scraps
low-born, arrogant, superficial
having only three changes of clothes — a servingman's standard wardrobe, implying low status
poor gentleman — barely qualified for the class
in cheap wool stockings rather than silk
cowardly — the liver was thought the seat of courage; pale liver = no blood there
one who sues at law rather than fighting
son-of-a-whore, vain (always looking in the mirror)
ready to do any service, however degrading
over-finicky; fussy
owning only one trunk's worth of possessions
procurer; pimp
nothing but moonlight-soaked — Kent threatens to beat Oswald into a bread-sop
the wooden leg-restraint in which Kent will be put — a punishment for commoners, a grave insult to a king's messenger
young man — ironic address
foolishly bowing servants
appearance; manner
the sun-god's forehead — astrological / mythological figure
the punishment bench — putting Kent in the stocks insults Lear directly
impudent incivility
the terrestrial sphere — Gloucester's astronomical figure
in the mud
invented proverbial enclosure — Kent's 'I'd beat you in any arena'
base; rascally
a bread-sop soaked in moonlight — Kent will beat Oswald into such a piece
a meat slashed for grilling — Kent threatens to cut Oswald's shanks that way

glosses.phrase: if I had thee in Lipsbury pinfold
glosses.definition: Kent's threat of a total beating anywhere he could corner Oswald
do you smile at my words (Oswald smirks; Kent rages)
Kent's reference — compared to these villains, the Homeric Ajax looks foolish
the sun-god's brow — Kent mocking Cornwall's pompous oath by dialect
a place name in the North — Kent's deliberately obscure location
respect due to age — the kind Kent's stocking insults
exhausted

annotations.title: Kent's catalogue of abuse — the Jacobean insult taxonomy
annotations.body: Kent's explosion of epithets at Oswald — 'a knave; a rascal; an eater of broken meats; a base, proud, shallow, beggarly, three-suited, hundred-pound, filthy, worsted-stocking knave; a lily-liver'd, action-taking, whoreson, glass-gazing, super-serviceable, finical rogue' — is a Jacobean comic aria, almost a list-poem of social insult. Each term has specific class-bite: 'three-suited' (a servingman's limit), 'worsted-stocking' (cheap hose rather than silk), 'lily-liver'd' (no blood in the liver = coward). The joke is that Kent is a nobleman in disguise unloading courtly vitriol on a courtier-servant. Oswald is, in Shakespeare's hierarchy, the worst kind of creature — a servant who takes on the manners and betrayals of the master-class without the obligations. Kent's verbal violence in this scene is itself a moral gesture; he is refusing to treat Oswald politely, which is the system's first rule.
sources: Russ McDonald, Shakespeare and the Arts of Language (2001)

annotations.title: Kent in the stocks — a symbolic assault on the king
annotations.body: Putting Kent (as 'Caius,' Lear's messenger) in the stocks is legally a minor punishment for a servant who strikes another. Symbolically it is an unprecedented insult to Lear: the king's messenger is the king in microcosm, and stocking him is stocking him. Gloucester recognizes this — 'The king must take it ill / That he, so slightly valued in his messenger, / Should have him thus restrain'd' — but Cornwall and Regan are past caring. Shakespeare marks the shift: politeness to Lear is no longer possible for them, and they are willing to have the court see it. The stocks in 2.2 are where the public phase of Lear's humiliation begins.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

trials.prompt: What does Kent's elaborate string of insults to Oswald accomplish?
options: A Jacobean taxonomy of class insult — each term has specific social bite; a nobleman (in disguise) unloading courtly vitriol on a servant who has taken on master-class manners

trials.prompt: Why is putting Kent in the stocks so serious an affront?
options: As Lear's messenger, Kent represents the king symbolically — stocking him is a deliberate public insult to the king himself
